data.title: EZA: Pressure Likely To Persist
data.description:
South Africa is a volatile nation that's grossly misunderstood by many, providing the necessary latitude for analysts to uncover overlooked events. We last covered the iShares MSCI South Africa ETF (NYSEARCA:EZA) shortly after South Africa's national election, which saw it settle into Government of National Unity. Many were optimistic about the GNU; however, we casted our doubts.

In an updated thesis we revisit South Africa's country risk and recent shifts in EZA's attributes to deliver an opinion about the vehicle's interim outlook; let's get started.

Rising Sovereign & Country Risk

Assessments of sovereign and country risk are correlated, yet possess independencies. The latter emphasises real economic factors, whereas the prior delves into market-based variables to judge the status of a nation's sovereign debt.

Starting with sovereign debt, South African bonds experience splendid form in late 2024 as lower inflation and a pivot in government led to lower yields, concurrently releasing untapped value. However, as expanded upon later, the U.S. has 'hit' South Africa with tariffs ever since and instability in the GNU has revealed itself. As a result, the South African 10-year yield is priced roughly 201.40 basis points higher than six months ago.

A longer term view illustrates that South Africa's sovereign spread (the difference between the U.S. 10-Y and SA's 10-Y) has widened considerably in the past seven years. While higher sovereign spreads can signal an enhanced real growth outlook, South Africa's CDS spread (an outlook of sovereign risk established by the CDS market) has widened in conjunction. Moreover, GDP growth has flatlined to near 0% simultaneously.

Date Sovereign Spread CDS Spread
2 April 2018 6.03% 150.50 bps
4 April 2022 4.97% 209.39 bps
7 April 2025 6.26% 244.25 bps

Source: Author's Calculations (Rounded), Data from Trading Economics & World Government Bonds

So, South Africa' sovereign risk is higher but what does all this mean for ETA ETF?

South Africa's higher sovereign risk is paired with elevated country risk, meaning financial market risk premiums might be assigned. In addition, lower nominal earnings might occur, driven by both internal and external pressures; herewith are a few factors worth considering.

  • South Africa recently passed an expropriation without compensation bill, threatening the rights of property owners. In our view, the bill extends into non-residential property, including intellectual property such as mining rights and/or business ownership rights. Elevated expropriation risk might debase foreign investor confidence, and lower company capital expenditures, in turn, compressing EZA's nominal earnings outlook.
  • South Africa's GNU has shown signs of instability. One of the primary reasons why stocks surged in late 2024 was due to a pivot away from an ANC government. However, anecdotes suggest the ANC remains firmly in control, despite ruling as a minority.
  • The United States has targeted South Africa with adverse rhetoric and heavy tariffs, mostly due to the ANC's pro-Palestine and anti-U.S. stance. Though President Trump has suspended reciprocal tariffs, we argue severed trade relations will sustain for an extended period.

Key Sectors Are Under Pressure

The following diagram parts EZA's sectoral exposure. The vehicle uses a market-cap approach to sample South Africa's Johannesburg Stock Exchange, meaning EZA is a thematic ETF instead of an excess return-seeking fund. It's illustrated that EZA ETF is overweight financials, basic materials, and communications through stocks such as Gold Fields (GFI), Anglogold Ashanti PLC (AU), MTN Group (OTCPK:MTNOY), Standard Bank Group (OTCPK:SBGOF), and other local market leaders. In addition, EZA's most substantial holding is Naspers (OTCPK:NPSNY), which operates globally with telling exposure to the EU via Prosus (OTCPK:PROSY) - In essence, Naspers isn't a South African pure play.

Unfortunately, we see headwinds unfolding for some of South Africa's key sectors; here's a breakdown.

  • PGMs: EZA maintains light exposure to platinum group metals mining, including Impala Platinum (OTCQX:IMPUY) and Anglo American Platinum (OTCPK:ANGPY). PGMs are under significant pressure amid a drop in palladium demand caused by consumers shifting towards electric vehicles. While PGMs comprise a small portion of EZA's portfolio (just over 3%), a drawdown in PGM stocks detracts from broad-based market capitalisation in South Africa, which could have an impact.
  • Other Resource Companies May Struggle: Companies such as Sasol (SSL) and Kumba Iron Ore (OTCPK:KUMBF) might face pressure if global trade slows. Although South Africa exports much of its resources to China, a trade war could deplete the global flow of goods and services; relocation of commodity sales would be challenging.
  • Consumer Goods Companies Vs. Consumer Sentiment: South Africa has faced negative consumer sentiment for an extended period. Moreover, factors such as a lacklustre employment rate and a newly introduce VAT hike could diminish consumer stocks' prospects.

  • Communications Firms Face Local Infrastructure Problems: Communication companies form a large part of EZA's portfolio. South Africa's electricity concerns and general infrastructure decay might eventually dent companies such as MTN and Vodacom (OTCPK:VODAF).

Though we expect broad-based pressure, counter-arguments exist. For example, Gold prices are steady and we believe additional rallies are possible during a time when investors could hedge against global economic tail risk. Although global trade uncertainties might impact gold exports, we see gold prices being a ballast, allowing companies such as Gold Fields and Harmony Gold (who partially operate abroad) to thrive. In addition, Naspers provides foreign tech sector exposure, decoupling from our systematic argument of South Africa.

EZA ETF's Key Metrics - High Dividend, Yet Slow Growth,

We compared EZA ETF to other emerging market ETFs, including iShares MSCI Mexico ETF (EWW), iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA), iShares MSCI Brazil ETF (EWZ), iShares MSCI China ETF (MCHI), and iShares MSCI Chile ETF (ECH). Other examples can be used; however, we deem this peer set prudent as a base for total return analysis.

In reality, EZA doesn't stack up poorly versus its peers from a valuation point of view with its price-to-earnings and price-to book ratios in line with the others. However, South Africa is a cyclical and slow growth economy. For example, we stated earlier that the country has faced near zero GDP growth in the past decade, which contrasts with a nation such a India, which has scaled above 6% in recent years. While foreign-operated EZA companies can drag EZA's real earnings growth upward, we see South Africa's local operators creating an overhang.

ETF P/E P/B
EZA 12.86x 1.78x
EWW 11.30x 1.53x
INDA 23.67x 3.76x
EWZ 8.83x 1.42x
MCHI 13.67x 1.67x
ECH 11.33x 1.20x

Source: iShares

EZA's trailing yield of 6.87% is compelling relative to those of its peers, yet, its yield has experienced negative five year compound annual growth. We think EZA might present a short-term dividend opportunity; however, future growth remains of concern, especially to foreign investors with tax implication.

A technical vantage point shows that EZA is trading below its 10-, 50-, 100-, and 200-day moving averages. In addition, the EZA presents a sub-50 RSI, suggesting it has room to roam into. Even though a technical argument carries gravity, we'd be careful of committing to it in isolation.

Conclusion

We see little support arising for the iShares MSCI South Africa ETF in the next twelve months and possibly beyond. Systematic support from gold miners and Naspers likely added a tailwind to EZA, which, combined with a GNU government resulted in robust index performance. However, we think the rest of EZA's portfolio will eventually create an overhang when gold prices and Naspers' interim growth normalise.

Consensus: Hold/Market Underperform/Underweight

data.title: Corning: A Materials Giant Playing The Long Game
data.description:
Introduction

Today, Corning (NYSE:GLW) is not just glass, it's the optical fiber through which data center traffic runs, it's the protective coatings of our smartphones, it's the materials in microscopes and catalytic filters in cars. Their business covers five areas at once: optical communications, display technologies, special materials (including the iconic Gorilla Glass), solutions for automotive ecology and laboratory equipment. In addition, the company develops polysilicon for solar panels through Hemlock and pharmaceutical packaging under the Valor brand.

In 2021, the company is setting a revenue record after a severe pandemic year. 2024 was a really strong year for the company: amid a surge in interest in fiber and a recovery in demand for advanced materials, revenue added 4% to reach $13.1 billion and EBITDA rose to $2.74 billion. Now Corning continues to increase its operational power. The company has successfully implemented a price increase for display glass, restored margins and began to actively invest in growth. In my opinion, Corning enters this stage with a very balanced model: it has a clear product focus, sound capital investment management and a strong financial foundation.

Business model

Corning Incorporated is an entire technological ecosystem that is able to transform deep scientific developments into real products used in our daily lives. Everything the company does is tied into five key areas, and we will start with the most ambitious one — optical communications. Corning literally stood at the origins: it was here that the world's first low-attenuation fiber appeared, and the company is still setting the pace for the market. Its solutions, from SMF-28 fibers to Evolv and EDGE platforms, are the foundation for next-generation telecom infrastructure, including 5G networks and hyperscale data centers.

The second most important block display technology. Corning invented the fusion process, which makes it possible to produce perfectly smooth sheets of glass, and this is a must for 4K/8K TVs. Astra Glass, Lotus NXT are what giants like Samsung and LG are working on. It accounts for 27% of the total revenue. Gorilla Glass is a protective glass that has become the industry standard for smartphones, watches, and tablets. The Victus® 2 version, for example, easily survives a fall on concrete. In addition, Corning makes unique glasses for the chip industry, AR/VR and defense industries.

The fourth area is environmental technologies. Corning has shaped this market since the 70s and today supplies ceramic filters to almost all leading automakers. Yes, electric cars are coming, but so far tens of millions of cars with internal combustion engines are released worldwide every year — its products are needed. This is about 12% of the total revenue. The fifth segment is Life Sciences. Glass and plastic for laboratories under the brands Corning®, PYREX®, Falcon® are the standard for biotech and pharma. There was a peak here in 2020-2021 due to COVID, then a recession, and now everything has stabilized. Approximately 7% of the total structure.

Corning's approach to customer service is so special that Apple invests in Corning factories in advance to ensure materials for future devices. The company strives to conclude long-term contracts and jointly develop new technologies.

Development strategy

The new strategic plan “Springboard" sets clear goals: the company is seriously aiming to accelerate revenue and achieve a 20% operating margin, and, importantly, in the first year it is not just going according to plan, but confidently exceeding it. The target for additional revenue was +$3 billion, but it was quickly raised to +$4 billion amid the dynamics in optical solutions for data centers, where there is now a real boom fueled by the growth of generative AI.

The company competently monetizes its innovations: expanding the Gorilla Glass line in electronics, entering the solar energy industry through the Solar MAP platform and strengthening its position in premium pharmaceutical packaging. All this is happening against the background of internal transformation: strict cost control, increased efficiency at all levels, and a balanced investment policy.

Competitors

Corning participates in several high-tech niches at once, holds strong positions and confidently defends them. In optical communications, it is among the world leaders, covering the entire cycle, from the fiber itself to ready-made solutions. The global fiber optic cable market was valued at USD 13 billion in 2024 and is estimated to grow at a CAGR of 10.4% to USD 34.5 billion in 2034, according to the Global Market Insights. The fiber optic cable industry is highly competitive featuring established global leaders as well as various regional startups. The key players in the global market are Corning, Sumitomo Electric Industries Ltd, Prysmian Group, Furukawa Electric (OTCPK:FUWAY), and Huawei Technologies, collectively accounting for over 40% of the total market share. In 2024, sales towards corporate networks took off, and this is understandable — we need more speed, more connectivity due to the growing demand for AI infrastructure. Amid all the talk about duties and tariffs, Corning's optical segment is barely suffering — everything key is made in the United States and Mexico, and if protectionism increases, the company will only strengthen its position at home.

The story is different with the display direction: The market itself is mature, and demand is not growing explosively, but Corning manages to stay one step ahead. Corning continues to dictate conditions — for example, in 2024 company was able to raise prices, and competitors simply had to tighten up, because there is simply no similar production of Gen 10+ glass. This market is an oligopoly: Corning, Japan's AGC (OTCPK:ASGLY) and Nippon Electric Glass (OTCPK:NPEGF) control more than 90% of the global supply of glass substrates. It is estimated by Business Research and Insights that the global market for glass substrates for LCD displays is about $9-10 billion per year and will grow modestly, by an average of 1-3% per year in the coming years (mainly due to larger panel sizes and the development of OLED technology).Yes, possible US tariffs on Chinese electronics are a potential disadvantage, because most of the screens are made in Asia. But given how flexible supply chains are now, this is more likely to lead to a shift in assembly to other countries than to a reduction in demand for Corning glass itself.

The special materials segment is, of course, about the power of the Gorilla Glass brand. It is used by almost all flagship smartphones. But it's not just about smartphones — there are also materials for the chip industry, AR/VR, optics. Here, the competition is not on price, but on quality, and Corning is clearly ahead. Temporary drops in demand, as it was in smartphones, the company is quietly experiencing due to a wide product line.

In automotive ceramics, Corning works in conjunction with Japanese NGK — their duopoly is almost indestructible. Life Sciences and new directions like pharmaceutical glass and solar polysilicon are rather point stories, but very promising. Here, Corning either gains a foothold in the market, or uses the “windows of opportunity” that have opened, for example, when the United States begins to localize production and squeeze out Chinese imports.

Financials

In 2024, revenue amounted to $13.1 billion, (+4.2% YoY). At the same time, the growth in constant prices turned out to be about 7%, because in 2023, a weak yen and other exchange rates underestimated dollar revenue. The main drivers are a sharp rise in optics (+16%) and steady growth in specialty products (+8%), while displays showed only a modest recovery.

With this foundation in mind, I plan to generate revenue of $15.7 billion in 2025, which implies a 12.5% increase in core sales. As in 2024, I expect the main contribution from optics, against the background of continued investments in AI infrastructure. Special materials, including a new generation of Gorilla Glass and optics for semiconductors, are also pulling up — I expect $2.2–2.3 billion. For displays, the growth will be more restrained — about 5-7%, mainly due to the effect of higher prices. The Eco-sector will stabilize, and Life Sciences will add about 5% in my opinion. My forecast is generally consistent with the consensus forecast, which expects revenue in 2025 in the range of $15.04-15.81 billion.

In 2024, Corning showed a steady EBITDA recovery: at the GAAP level, this figure rose to $2.48 billion (+10% yoy), and if we look at the core metric, it reached $2.74 billion, which is closer to real operational efficiency. The operating margin reached about 10.57%. It is especially important that the company was able to keep cost growth under control and began to remove the effect of operating leverage: the gross margin rose to 34.2%, and expenses hardly grew.

I expect EBITDA in the region of $3.6 billion and margin recovery to 23% by the levels of 2021-2022. This is the result of several factors at once: an increase in volumes in optics, a stable yen, a high load of glass furnaces, and cost control. According to my model, net profit could reach $950 million, which is almost twice as high as in 2024. At the same time, the consensus forecast is more optimistic for net profit growth in 2025, expecting EPS of about $2.36, which means a net profit of over $1.5 billion.

By the end of 2024, Corning's debt burden looks moderate: the total interest-bearing debt is $7.2 billion, of which only $326 million is due to be repaid in the coming year. Net debt is about $5.44 billion, which gives a Net Debt/EBITDA ratio of about 2.3x, based on core EBITDA of $2.74 billion. And most importantly, the company continues to repay the debt without resorting to additional borrowing, and the entire structure is strongly biased towards long—term issues: the average term is 23 years, which gives a comfortable distance to repayment. Even at the current rate of 4.1% (the estimated average for the entire debt), interest payments do not exert pressure: EBITDA covers them almost eightfold.

After CapEx's active growth in 2020-2022, when the company was building new facilities for Gorilla Glass and optics, in 2024 CapEx was sharply reduced to $965 million — just 7.4% of revenue, which was the lowest in several years. This gave a significant boost to free cash flow. But in 2025, everything is turning towards growth again. The company plans to increase investments to $1.3 billion, which is about 8% of my projected revenue of $15.7 billion. The main focus will be on optical fiber, Gorilla Glass and, possibly, the launch of new directions like Solar MAP. The investment level is balanced: it allows you to scale production without overheating. In fact, Corning is moving into organic growth mode with a reasonable investment rhythm in the range of 8-10% of revenue.

Corning showed impressive growth to $1.21 billion in 2024. The reason is an increase in margins and a sharp reduction in capital investments from $1.39 billion to $965 million. By 2025, I expect the free flow to remain strong, about $2 billion. Corning will again actively produce "for growth," and part of the FCF will go into inventory and accounts receivable. Nevertheless, even adjusted for this, the company will continue to confidently generate a cache. This allows you to save dividends, partially buy back shares and invest in growth without putting pressure on the balance sheet.

Corning has long adhered to a stable and predictable dividend policy — the annual increase in payments has become a tradition. In 2024, the dividend per share was $1.12 with a yield of about 2.59%. In 2025, according to the market consensus forecast, the company will allocate about $1.035 billion in dividends or $1.19 per share with a yield of 2.84%. At the same time, Corning resumed buyback: in 2024, it took about $250 million, and the total limit of the buyback program remains high — $3.1 billion.

Valuation

The company's shares have grown strongly by 30.49% over the past year, but the growth would have been higher if not for the fall of the entire stock market in April 2025 after the news of the imposition of tariffs by Trump. At the same time, the S&P500 grew by only 2.08%. I believe that after the April correction, the company's shares have formed an upside in price of 10-11%, which can be used.

The company trades at a premium to its historical averages and medians in terms of multipliers: the premium for P/E is 53%, and for EV/EBITDA is about 19%.

Compared to peers, the company trades at small premiums to the average market values in terms of P/E, P/B, P/S and EV/EBITDA multipliers, as shown in the table below.

In my forecast of the target price, I use four estimation methods: DCF, target value of P/E, P/S and target value of EV/EBITDA with weights in the estimate of 25%. In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3.0% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 7.75%. I chose TGR based on the long-term global economic growth rate of 2-3% per year. Equity risk premium is taken from the Damodaran table. The current 10-Year Treasury Rate is used as the risk-free rate.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 48.73x, P/S is 2.75x, and EV/EBITDA is 13.40x (I took the historical averages, assuming that the high multipliers will decrease significantly in 2025 to at least the historical averages).

According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $46.08 with a 10.52% growth potential to the current price. The total return, together with the paid dividend for 2025, could be about 13.38%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating. Wall street analysts are slightly less conservative in their price forecasts, but the overall view of the company is similar – the consensus expects a price of $54.92 with an upside of about 31.73%.

Key risks

The main risk for Corning is its dependence on the general state of the economy and technological cycles. When investors invest less in telecom, electronics, or the auto industry, it immediately affects Corning. We already saw this in 2023, when a pause in the capex of telecom operators hit the optical direction, and this may well happen again. There are also political stories that Corning has to consider: if the United States dramatically tightens the screws with tariffs on electronics from China, this could lead to a drawdown in display production — and hence demand for glass. Well, do not forget about the trend for electric vehicles — it is slowly but surely eroding one of Corning's established sources of income in automotive ceramics. Plus, there is price competition in laboratory products, and this can gradually eat up margins. Well, and geopolitics. China accounts for about a quarter of Corning's total revenue, and trade wars, conflicts, and escalations between major countries can severely affect the company's business.

Key growth drivers

One of the most powerful drivers is telecom and data centers. In 2024, Corning's corporate optics sales soared by 93%, and everything indicates that this growth will continue: the AI and cloud infrastructure requires more and more speed, connections, and reliability, and Corning is among the technology leaders here. Another interesting growth lever is Solar MAP and pharmaceutical glass. Both topics fit into trends: renewable energy and localization of production (plus biopharma against the background of R&D growth) open up the opportunity for Corning to scale into new niches. Displays began to recover, and Corning has shown that it is able to maintain margins even in a stagnant market, due to a sound pricing policy.

Conclusion

Corning's financial condition at the end of 2024 is strong, with a tendency to improve. Revenue has returned to growth and, according to my expectations, will accelerate in 2025, profitability is recovering: pricing and cost-saving measures have raised margins in 2024. Given the trends in telecom, electronics, and renewable energy, the company's prospects are really serious. According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of the company's shares is $46.08 with a 10.52% growth potential to the current price. The total return, together with the dividends paid for 2025, may amount to about 13.38%. That's why I give the company a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Capri Holdings: Neutral Rating Post-Versace Deal
data.description:
Following our recent sell rating on Capri Holdings (NYSE:CPRI), 'A Perfect Storm Ahead - This Is A Sell,' which worked out very well (shares declined by more than 43% in 2 months - Fig 1). Today, we are back to comment on the company after the Versace disposal. Last time, our pivotal question around the company was how much Wall Street had already priced in a potential Versace sale in the stock price. To address this question, we analyzed what Capri Holding's pro forma profits might look like post-deal and considered what multiple shares could be traded post-deal.

Fig 1

Versace-Prada Deal Review

In 2019, the company acquired Versace for approximately $2.12 billion. Yesterday, Prada Group announced that it has agreed to purchase 100% of Versace with an enterprise value of $1.375 billion, with a transaction financed by approximately €1.5 billion in new debt. The transaction is expected to close in H2 2025 and is subject to customary closing conditions, such as antitrust. Even if we were reporting a solid financial position from Prada, we were not speculating on a potential acquisition from the Prada management team, which appeared focused on organic growth acceleration and mainly backed by expanding the Miu Miu portfolio. Related to Prada, we see the deal in a positive light as Italy's response to French conglomerates (Kering and LVMH) from a group that has successfully turned around two brands and outperformed the industry in the post-COVID-19 outbreak.

Regarding Capri Holding, last time, we reported a 30% discount on a Price to Sales multiple on Versace (and Jimmy Choo PLC), which was even worse than anticipated. Versace's acquisition price is equivalent to an EV/Sales multiple of 1.5x (based on 2024 top-line sales), compared to the 20-year average in the sector of approximately 3.0x. We should also report that Prada Group considers the Versace brand a "strongly complementary addition to the Prada Group's portfolio and displays significant untapped growth potential." This was also true with the potential Capri Holdings' upside to create a luxury conglomerate that failed to materialize.

Capri Holdings Upside and Financial Estimate

Three key reasons support Capri Holdings' short-term view. Indeed, selling Versace would likely be significantly accretive to the company's earnings per share for various reasons (Fig 2):

  1. EBIT Margin benefit since Versace's LTM EBIT margin was -4.6% vs. Capri Holdings' 4.4%;
  2. The company could use the deal proceeds to reduce its financial leverage and lower its ongoing interest expense;
  3. Additionally, given the negative share price evolution in the last two years, the company could announce a share buyback with the deal proceeds.

Fig 2

Post Versace deal, we anticipate 2026 pro-forma NTM top-line sales of $3.29 billion and an EBITDA of $315 million. In our assumption, we previously forecasted Versace to generate sales for the fiscal year 2026 (ending in March 2026) of approximately $800 million. This follows a sales growth rate of -8 % and an EBITDA growth rate of -5 %. Last time, we reported a positive gross margin evolution, but Capri Holding plans to reduce its stores by 75 locations, limiting retail sales recovery. Additionally, the company will move on with promotional activity to clear its inventory. In our estimates, with Versace proceeds, the company will become debt free, so there is at least an interest saving of $60 million annually. As of Q3 2024, the company had approximately $1.48 billion in outstanding total debt, including a revolving credit facility. With a core EBIT margin of 6% and a tax rate of 15%, Capri Holding's net income is projected at $146 million, with an EPS of $1.41. As a reminder, the company pays relatively low taxes due to a combination of strategic tax planning and tax-efficient structures. Indeed, the company is incorporated in the British Virgin Islands, a jurisdiction known for favorable tax laws. Based on the FT share count, we use 117.89 million shares in our earnings-per-share calculation.

Valuation

Regarding the valuation methodology, we think a good peer group for Capri Holding is G-III Apparel Group, Levi Strauss & Co, PVH Corp, and Tapestry. We also consider that the company is in a restructuring phase with negative top-line sales trend expectations (and a lack of profit from Jimmy Choo, too). After the Versace transaction, the company shares are trading at a 10.4x P/E multiple. This group's 2026 P/E range trade is between 5.5x and 18x, with a current EV/EBITDA range of 4.3x and 12x. If we believe on a pro-forma basis, Capri Holding would trade slightly in the middle of these ranges; the shares look pretty valued today at $14. Last time, we also used a 10x P/E multiple aligned with the share's average multiple since the Tapestry deal announcement in August 2023. We moved Capri Holding to neutral on a 12-month view compared to our last rating.

Risks

We have extensive coverage of Capri Holdings, so we report the following risks: 1) change in consumer preference, 2) ongoing restructuring in Kors location and potential Jimmy Choo PLC trade sales, 3) ability to innovate with compelling product trends, 4) logistics risks and trade war implication with its footprint industrial facility, 5) lower consumer spending levels in the accessories/handbags category, 6) FX changes (45% of the sales are not denominated in US dollar).

Conclusion

Last time, we reported a perfect storm ahead, with a valuation multiple of 15x P/E and an imbalance of risk/rewards. After the Versace deal, we believe Capri Holding could use excess cash proceeds to restructure the company, pay down debt, and reduce its outstanding shares. This should drive its EPS by high single digits. Therefore, Capri Holding is now correctly priced.

data.title: Globe Life: Share Repurchases Masking Mediocre Growth, But Still Compares Well To Peers
data.description:
One year ago I wrote an article on Globe Life Inc. (NYSE:GL), defending the stock after an attack from a short seller. At that time the stock was trading around $60 and I was betting on a bounce to $80. Fast forward to today and it appears that the attack from the short seller is in the rear view mirror. Despite a recent pullback, the stock sits at nearly double my initial purchase point and 50% higher than my target. So now is a good time to reassess if GL is still worth a buy at this level.

Unlike other instances where short reports often cause disruption to a company's operations and brand, GL looks like it wasn't impacted at all in 2024. Reviewing the company's annual report, its life insurance premium revenues grew from $3.14 billion to $3.26 billion from 2023 to 2024, a 4% increase. That compares favorably to the 3.6% growth rate seen from 2022 to 2023 as 2022 revenue was $3.03 billion. Health premium revenue grew 6.5% from $1.32 billion to $1.4 billion from 2023 to 2024. That grow rate was significantly better than the 2.8% in growth seen from 2022 to 2023 as 2022 revenue was $1.28 billion.

These single-digit growth rates are what you would expect in a mature industry. But GL bought back 10.1 million shares last year. This was a significant factor behind earnings per share growing an impressive 19% from $10.07 in 2023 to $11.94 in 2024. The share repurchases resulted in weighted average shares outstanding dropping from 96.3 million in 2023 to 89.7 million in 2024. GL currently has about 84 million shares outstanding today so we can expect the declining denominator to further impact EPS growth for 2025, even without further share repurchases.

The company is forecasting net operating income per share of $13.45 to $14.05 for 2025. Net operating income per share for 2024 was $12.37, so the $13.75 midpoint leads to a solid 11% growth. The company likes to highlight that this growth is due to favorable mortality trends and a stable underwriting performance. But in reality the majority of the growth is due to the declining share count. I estimate that 7% of the delta is due to that with actual earnings growth accounting for the remaining 4%.

This aggressive share repurchase strategy is causing GL's growth grade to be wonky:

On a nominal basis, GL struggles to come in line with the sector median, but its growth rate on EPS numbers is more than double the growth rate of its peers.

I feel that GL's valuation grade of D+ is unfair. GL is about 20% cheaper compared to its sector median on all valuation metrics except one. Its price to book value metric which is about 70% higher than the sector median. Given that GL is in the insurance industry, there is relatively more emphasis on book value multiples than in other industries such as technology. My opinion is that GL clearly is able to operate well under a relatively asset-light model. I put more weight into the earnings multiples.

While the stock trades at a book value premium relative to its peers, GL is trading in line to its historical book value multiple and at a discount to where it was in 2023. The revenue multiple of just under 2x is trading at a slight discount to where it has been historically. The earnings multiple of 10x is trading below historical norms of 12x or more.

The divergence of the book value multiple and revenue/earnings multiples is a consequence of the aggressive share repurchase plan. As shares are purchased, the EPS increases in a manufactured way. Investors will catch on that EPS growth is outpacing revenue growth due to a shrinking share count, so they won't base their purchase solely on the EPS figure. As cash is used to buy back shares, GL's business becomes even more asset light, thereby increasing the P/BV ratio as cash and shares are extinguished at a dollar-for-dollar ratio. Let me illustrate with a simple example. Assume a company has a $200 million market cap and $100 million in shareholder's equity. It trades at 2x book. It then uses $50 million in cash to buy back shares and has $50 million in shareholder's equity remaining. If all else is equal, the market cap drops to $150 million and now it trades at 3x book. But the business has not fundamentally changed, as long as the cash was not needed for growth investment.

Conclusion: A mixed bag for GL. Bulls on the company can continue to hold, but I am no longer interested in this stock

I think GL is justified in having a book value that trades well above industry averages. Its relatively asset-light business model is working. However, it's masking its comparatively mediocre growth rate by buying back shares then attributing that growth to actual strength in the business. That to me is a red flag.

On the flip side, if you ignore book value metrics, GL is trading at about a 20% discount to peers on revenue and earnings metrics. Looking at its historical trading levels, the earnings multiple of 10x is also trading at a 20% discount to a historical standard of 12x. It's not often that you see a company boasting of an expected 11% growth in EPS trading at 10x trailing P/E. Regardless of how the company managed to get there.

Investors who are bullish on GL can probably justify another 20% increase in the stock price. I wish them luck but based on these prices, the potential upside of GL is no longer of interest to me. I suggest a hold if you have shares already and a pass if you don't. Unless you have money burning a hole in your pocket that you absolutely must deploy in the market.

data.title: CEF Weekly Review: BlackRock Resets Its Tender Offers
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund ("CEF") market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the first week of April. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company ("BDC") as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

Nearly all sectors were down on the week with only Munis and Agencies up in NAV terms due to the sharp drop in Treasury yields. Equity-linked sectors struggled in particular.

The median CEF sector discount moved wider of its historical average.

The credit CEF swirlogram looks much better than it did earlier in the year. Readers who follow our swirlogram indicator are well aware that CEFs were very expensive by historic standards on the two key metrics (underlying asset valuations and discounts). This is the main reason why we have significantly pared our allocation to CEFs over the last 12-18 months. Now that valuations have derated sharply, we are willing to step back in and add positions.

Market Themes

BlackRock announced new CEF tender offers with respect to their discount management programs. Funds with triggered buybacks are BOE, BCX, BGY, BSTZ and MVF. Tender offers for these funds will begin on 16-17 April and will expire on 19-20 May. 2.5% of outstanding shares will be bought back at a 2% discount.

MVF is a decent Muni CEF option given the additional uplift of around 2% annualized. MVF remains in our Muni Income Portfolio. The uplift from the historic tender offers will not show up in its performance numbers but it’s there if investors participate in the tender offers.

Market Commentary

Not a ton of news on the CEF distribution front. BlackRock adjusted their managed distribution funds such as BSTZ, BTX, BMEZ, BCAT and ECAT. There were also tiny adjustments for Eaton Vance EVG and EVV. Few changes elsewhere.

Elsewhere, Brookfield has agreed to acquire the majority of Angel Oak Companies LP - the parent of the adviser of the bank bond CEF FINS. If the acquisition is successful, the investment advisory agreement will terminate and fund shareholders will need to approve a new agreement which is expected to be substantially similar to the previous one.

FINS is a mostly investment-grade financials bond fund with a low duration of around 2. The management fee is very high at 1.35% with additional operating expenses. Historical performance is unimpressive at around a 2-3% CAGR on a 5 and 10-year total NAV basis.

Stance and Takeaways

We are starting to see great value across a large number of CEFs including ARDC, JLS, CIK, PHK and others. We plan to add small positions across a number of funds to take advantage of the sharp improvement in valuations in the coming days.

Our "drier-powder" allocations such as Muni funds have held up very well, being flat or up on the year while many credit CEFs are down 5-15%. This offers an opportunity to both improve the yield of our Income Portfolios as well as to take advantage of the sell-off.

data.title: Endesa Value Proposition Beat Out By Parent Enel
data.description:
Endesa, S.A. (OTCPK:ELEZF) (OTCPK:ELEZY) had some pretty positive developments since our last coverage. The Spanish government has signed a non-law document urging the government to seriously reconsider plans to decommission its major nuclear footprint, and in particular to make sure that it's also economically viable to run them. In our previous coverage there was the news that one of their more irrelevant sites had received a 10-year life extension but not their major assets. This may change as a bill sponsored by the conservatives will push for keeping nuclear going as long as possible rather than decommissioning into the 2030s as previously planned in 2019, pending reviews that the government has been urged to do with the CNMC and the municipalities. Business leaders have voiced interest in keeping the assets alive, although it seems dependent on a review of the taxes imposed on nuclear as continuing to run nuclear assets at this point is not that attractive. A serious review of the plan to start shutting down the bulk of the footprint in the 2030s still hasn't occurred. The benefits aren't overwhelming if the nuclear footprint does see a life extension, but they aren't irrelevant either as at least extension of life investments have a higher NPV than new construction, despite the high costs of sustaining nuclear beyond commissioned life. Other than that, Endesa performance was decent with good EBITDA growth despite continued windfall taxes and lower electricity prices, with the inflation-hedged dynamics of networks businesses lending a hand to results.

Endesa Results

All the segments saw decent performance. In networks, the adjustments of the remuneration parameters to better reflect the cost of capital environment were a support to the networks results, posting a double-digit EBITDA increase despite the segment's non-volatile character. There was also good cost control in networks despite the tendency for these businesses to be troubled, particularly now, by limited labour supplies and labour inflation.

In generation, there was solid incremental performance from CCGT thanks to the more normalised gas environment and cheapening gas input prices. In other words, the spark spread has improved despite a double-digit decline in electricity prices of 28%. Also, last year was severely affected by an arbitration where Endesa had to pay significant sums to QatarEnergy. Growth here was despite shrinking contribution from thermal production, which is generally higher margin. Of help was the general increase in capacity, and decent performances in hydroelectric, a major source, with 36% higher production and decent reservoirs full by 56%. Actual capacity increased by 45% YoY in the renewable business.

Other news is that the investments in solar and wind are expected to slow down as discipline requires more selectivity in developing or acquiring assets. This is interesting because it reflects a similar situation to EDP (OTCPK:ELCPF) which is slowing down asset rotations and expecting to be able to generate considerable incremental free cash flow to support dividends and deleveraging. Endesa is half as leveraged as EDP, but with low cash conversions and the prospects of needing to possibly invest to sustain the nuclear footprint, we don't think that a slowdown in investment into new capacity is a major issue for investors and may raise free cash conversion currently at 20% of EBITDA. Since it will take time for reduced investments at the top of the pipe to make its way into slower production growth and EBITDA growth, we don't think the effects on results will be imminent and that there will be a short-term earnings concern.

Bottom-Line

The hit to oil and gas markets as the Trump tariffs affected recession forecasts means that associated electricity prices, which have a relation to gas prices due to extensive use of CCGT, have also trailed down sequentially. The comps are going to toughen year-on-year if electricity prices stay at these levels. This will be a headwind.

However, the bigger issue we have is on valuation. While extension of nuclear would be good news and support from at least the right-wing parties and business leaders is mounting, it hasn't changed the ultimate provisions to decommission the plants one day. Moreover, as explained, there are considerable investments required to keep nuclear going beyond its commissioned life that are not that different in quantum to the decommissioning costs - even though it has been said that the nuclear facilities are already modernised and able to operate for many decades more in the recent nuclear manifesto, not signed by Endesa. Enel (OTCPK:ENLAY) which doesn't have this burden, trades at a PE and EV/EBITDA discount to Endesa which we do not believe is justified, as Enel, of course, the parent company of Endesa, carries these provisions as a much smaller percentage of their balance sheet.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Thoughts On JPMorgan's Call, Q2 '25 Guidance, Changes In S&P 500 EPS Estimates Will Get Attention
data.description:
JPMorgan (JPM) rose $9, or 4%, after reporting their Q1 ’25 financial results early Friday morning. The House that Jamie Built is seemingly the largest bank in America with the largest breadth across the consumer, corporate and capital markets, commercial banking and wealth management segments. Given the tariff uncertainty and the potential economic slowdown, and with 1/3rd of the year almost gone, 2025 could be a retrenchment year for the banks. (JPM earnings preview here.)

If readers get a chance, you should take a minute and read the JPMorgan earnings transcript. It’s 33 pages chock-full of great commentary on the health of the consumer and corporate mindset, as well as thoughts on a recession, as the tariff details are ironed out. Jamie Dimon pulls no punches on the regulatory issues, either. Dimon has been out front on behalf of JPM (and really the US banking system) for years on speaking against the onerous and costly regulatory burden, which the Treasury Secretary is trying to fix.

The JPMorgan call is a good segue into the S&P 500 earnings, since Jamie Dimon did comment on the call about S&P 500 earnings. On page 10 of the Q1 ’25 earnings transcript, Jamie Dimon noted that “... but the analyst community has already reduced its earnings estimates for the S&P 500 by 5%. So it’s now 5% as opposed to up 10%.” (Here’s the key quote), “My guess is that’ll be 0% and negative -5% probably next month.”

It doesn’t say whether Dimon is referring to Q1 ’25, Q2 ’25, or full-year 2025 in terms of the S&P 500 EPS estimates.

Here’s the trend in Q1 ’25 expected S&P 500 EPS growth by sector:

Here’s the trend in Q2 ’25 expected EPS growth by sector:

Trend in full-year expected S&P 500 EPS growth by sector:

(Table Source: LSEG “This Week in Earnings” 4/11/2025)

Of the three tables above, for the Q1 ’24 expected S&P 500 earnings period, the expected S&P 500 EPS growth rate has declined from 12.2% on January 1, ’25 to 8% on April 11, ’25, for a drop of 420 points, or 4.2%.

For the Q2 ’25 earnings period (middle table above), the Q2 ’25 expected S&P 500 EPS growth has fallen from 12% on January 1 ’25, to +9.2% as of April 11th, ’25, for a drop of 280 basis points, or 2.8%.

For full-year 2025 (third table), the drop from January 1, ’25 has been from 14% to 9.8%, or 220 basis points, or 2.2%.

Jamie Dimon didn’t say where he sourced his earnings data from, or when, so it could be tracked internally at JPMorgan. It could be a FactSet number or S&P Capital IQ number, or it might be an older LSEG quote.

The real point this blog wants to make to readers is that these above patterns (lower revisions into an earnings quarter and then an upside surprise after actual earnings begin to get reported) is the standard pattern for S&P 500 earnings estimates over the years. This blog has cautioned readers on this consistently many times. Ed Yardeni, the dean of the S&P 500 earnings forecasters, calls this the “fish-hook effect”. (Follow the pattern’s math and draw a line through the growth rates, and the line will resemble a backwards fish hook.)

Jamie Dimon could be referring to this normal pattern, not knowing it’s “the normal pattern”.

So far the downward revisions, as detailed above, are nothing unusual.

Summary / conclusion

Jamie Dimon is probably one of the top 10 CEOs in the S&P 500 the last 20 years. Remember, a lot of “earnings”-related commentary gets thrown out into the market frequently, but you have to be careful who is doing it and whether it’s realistic or not.

Frankly, Q1 ’25 actual S&P 500 EPS and revenue results will probably be ok, i.e., roughly in line with estimates, and after the quarter is all said and done, the “upside surprise” for Q1 ’25 S&P 500 will likely be 2-3% higher than the already expected +8% LSEG is expecting today. (See the first table inserted above.)

It’s the second-quarter ’25 which will likely feel the very conservative guidance that is expected from S&P 500 members. JPM’s conference call did say that the larger companies within the S&P 500 will likely weather this period better than the smaller companies. (Probably not a surprise to many.)

Q1 ’25 expected revenue growth per LSEG is calling for +4.1% revenue growth when Q1 ’25 has reported entirely. The S&P 500 revenue growth by quarter averaged 5% in 2024, and looking by quarter, from Q4 ’23 to Q1 ’12, the S&P 500 revenue growth has averaged +4.9%.

Which is more important - S&P 500 revenue growth or EPS growth? Always revenue growth, since S&P 500 EPS is a direct function of revenue.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investing can, and does, involve the loss of principal, even for short periods of time. LSEG is the source of all EPS and revenue estimates provided. It’s an occasional reminder, but after the last few weeks and the equity and bond market volatility we’ve seen, readers and all investors should gauge your own comfort with portfolio volatility.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ClearBridge Appreciation ESG Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Michael Kagan & Stephen Rigo, CFA

Economic Momentum At Risk

Market Overview

Despite a fast start to the year — a broad-based rally propelled the S&P 500 Index +2.8% in January — the market struggled for traction in the first quarter of 2025. Post-election animal spirits faded in February and March as investors grappled with on again/off again tariff announcements and consternation over the AI outlook. By the end of the quarter, the S&P declined -4.3%, breaking the index’s streak of five consecutive quarterly advances.

Outside of energy, the best-performing sector in the quarter, the market’s sector performance exhibited a defensive posture with health care, consumer staples, utilities and real estate the next-best performing industries. The energy sector outperformed as oil price stability provided air cover for stocks to recover from the deeply oversold level many shares entered the year. Within health care, investors found shelter in biotechnology and pharmaceutical shares as well as the beaten down managed care industry. Finally, regulated utilities and the real estate sector offered their usual haven status during choppy waters.

Technology bore the brunt of underperformance. Yes, the consumer discretionary sector appears as the market laggard (-13.8%), but this was primarily driven by downside in shares of Tesla (TSLA) and Amazon.com (AMZN), which declined -36% and -13%, respectively. The information technology (IT) sector underperformed the index in each month of the quarter, the first three-month losing streak for IT since August–October 2022. Although IT underperformance was broad-based, AI-levered names bore the worst of the selling as investors fretted about the impact of Chinese advancement in AI combined with the still uncertain ROI on significant capital outlays.

Outlook

The onset of the Trump administration’s tariff shock therapy caused a sharp decline in the first week of the new quarter and shifted the market dialogue. The last time the world saw tariffs this high was during the Great Depression after the U.S. implemented the Smoot-Hawley tariffs. We caution about drawing too close a parallel to that era. The U.S. and the world were entering a serious recession in 1929, and while the tariffs added fuel to the fire, they were by no means the major cause of the depression.

As the second quarter of 2025 begins U.S. economic fundamentals are benign. Current measures of liquidity are conducive for risky assets. Credit spreads remain tight by historic standards; the yield curve is positively sloped with risk-free rates relatively stable at benign levels; bank deposits are growing at an accelerated pace while loan underwriting standards ease; M&A and IPO activity is thawing; and the Fed plans to slow balance sheet runoff. An unstable economy like in 1929 is a lot easier to knock off kilter than the stability we see now.

"An unstable economy like in 1929 is a lot easier to knock off kilter than the stability we see now."

We view the tariff issue as two-pronged. First, the current lack of policy visibility creates uncertainty. While the announced tariffs are very large, their eventual level will be determined by international reaction and the Trump administration’s willingness to take the pain the tariffs will create. The uncertainty seems likely to create a slowdown (or pause) in corporate capital expenditures. How can we ask our business leaders to make multiyear, growth-creating, job-generating investments in property, plants or equipment without knowing whether these investments will be impacted by broad-based tariffs? Simply put, these multiyear investments require policy stability. Markets will be volatile until we know what the tariffs will be.

The second issue with tariffs is the potential impact on inflation, Fed policy and interest rates. We view broad-based tariffs as a one-time step-up in the price of impacted goods, which could be far reaching. That said, we worry more about the potential knock-on effects of tariffs. First, with lower-income consumers already stretched by high prices, goods inflation elevates the risk of slower consumer spending (recall consumer spend accounts for two-thirds of U.S. GDP). Americans will have to budget for the rising cost of necessities such as food at the expense of discretionary items such as travel and entertainment. Second, higher consumer prices could lead to another bout of wage inflation as people fight to have income keep pace with rising costs. If realized, these two knock-on effects could significantly change the U.S. economic backdrop.

In sum, while both our tariff concerns could be considered transitory or one-time in nature, the biggest risk in our view is the potential for lost economic momentum. Should capital investments go from deferred to delayed or consumers from optimistic to pessimistic, reigniting the engine of economic growth and investment may prove challenging.

Exhibit 1: World Food Prices

In addition to tariff uncertainties, the second potential risk we see to the markets involves the evolution of generative AI. Entering the year, expectations of the impact generative AI will have on economic growth were sky high despite lacking evidence of monetizable consumer applications. Agentic AI, a type of AI that can make decisions autonomously with limited human supervision, became a Silicon Valley buzzword but remains an efficiency tool more akin to table stakes for software services than a step function monetization opportunity.

In addition, the emergence of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), a China-based low-cost large language model, stoked fears about the returns investors should expect from the massive investment being made in the AI ecosystem. At a minimum, the DeepSeek revelation was a trigger event that shifted investor attention from chasing an open-ended dream to a more discerning incremental investment dollar. Investors are finally asking questions like: If models can be trained on lower-cost GPUs how many Nvidia clusters does the world need? If inference can be done on CPUs, what does that imply from a hardware ecosystem standpoint? How many large language models does the world need and what is the return potential from the proliferation of these models? We view investor focus on tangible ROIs following the initial AI gold rush phase as a painful but necessary transition akin to the rationalization Internet investors experienced in the late 1990s and early 2000s.

Conclusion

Our economic and market outlook has become somewhat binary in nature and incrementally more cautious. On the one hand, we believe if policy certainty comes soon current growth concerns could prove transitory with tariff impact perhaps manageable. On the other hand, the current “on again/off again” policy environment has meaningfully raised the risk of an economic slowdown, one that risks being concurrent with rising consumer prices, a potentially bad outcome for risky assets.

Although today the economic backdrop remains conducive for equities, we can no longer overweight this as a base case in our outlook. As such — and as risk-conscious investors focused on outperforming through a market cycle via downside protection — we believe incremental caution is warranted as we digest incoming information. Rather than trying to bet on near-term earnings trends or government policy outcomes, we believe it is better to look out two to three years and make investment decisions based upon our assessment of a company’s longer-term sustainable growth rate versus what’s implied in today’s share price. Given rising odds of a bumpy ride, we believe our Strategy is well-suited to the current environment.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Appreciation ESG Strategy outperformed the benchmark S&P 500 Index in the first quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy had positive contributions from seven of 11 sectors. The financials, health care and consumer staples sectors were the main positive contributors to performance, while IT, communication services and consumer discretionary were the main detractors.

In relative terms, stock selection in the financials and consumer discretionary sectors aided results, as did overweights to consumer staples and financials and underweights to IT and consumer discretionary. Stock selection in the IT, industrials and consumer staples sectors detracted the most.

On an individual stock basis, the biggest contributors to absolute performance during the quarter were Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B), Visa (V), Travelers Companies (TRV), Waste Management (WM) and AbbVie (ABBV). The biggest detractors were Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), Apple (AAPL) and Amazon.com.

During the quarter, we initiated new positions in Workday (WDAY) in the IT sector, Tesla in the consumer discretionary sector and WEC Energy (WEC) in the utilities sector. We exited Mondelez International (MDLZ) in the consumer staples sector, Comcast (CMCSA) in the communication services sector, Marvell Technology (MRVL) and Adobe (ADBE) in the IT sector and Sempra (SRE) in the utilities sector.

ESG Highlights: SDGs Key for Productivity and Growth

While some of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, or SDGs, consider elemental topics such as water, hunger, poverty, good health and education, and are therefore easier to understand and discuss, SDGs such as Decent Work and Economic Growth (SDG 8) and Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure (SDG 9) may be lesser known or understood. These emphasize the importance of sustainable development and are a focus for ClearBridge as they are among the SDGs most reliant on the private sector for progress.

Investing in Decent Work and Economic Growth

SDG 8 aims for decent work and economic growth, and includes promoting inclusive and sustainable economic growth, with employment and decent work for all. Among its targets (8.2) is achieving higher levels of economic productivity through diversification, technological upgrading and innovation, including a focus on high-value-added and labor-intensive sectors. Enterprise software aids in this goal, as the products and services of companies like ClearBridge holdings ServiceNow (NOW), Microsoft and Salesforce (CRM) are key for enhancing economic productivity. IT services companies such as Accenture (ACN) help companies implement technology upgrades.

Other examples of technology upgrading across industries include Amazon’s robotics-enabled warehouses, which are delivering speed increases that do not come at the expense of employee health, as we discussed in a recent engagement with the company. Amazon’s fulfilment center (FC) recordable injury rate (injuries considered exceeding first aid) is down 34% from 2019 to 2024 and 6% year over year and now stands at 4.4%. Lost-time injuries are down 65% over five years and 13% year over year, showing that safety investments are paying off. With 750,000 robots across fulfillment centers, FCs equipped with next-gen robotics have approximately 20% lower injury rates and lost-time injury rates than FCs without.

Amazon compares favorably to peers on its lost time incident rate (LTIR) and its recordable incident rate (RIR), with a LTIR better than the general warehousing and storage industry and courier and express delivery services industry averages and a RIR better than courier and express and that is quickly catching up to the general warehousing and storage average. Amazon has also implemented more shift types to offer flexibility, including an “anytime shift” where employees can block out a certain day of the week.

Other examples of technology upgrading include Deere’s (DE) precision agriculture offerings and connected combines, which are upgrading the technological capabilities of the agricultural sector, and Ecolab’s (ECL) digital service offerings for remote monitoring and analytics to improve operational efficiency of its customers.

Enabling Resilient Infrastructure and Advancing Research

SDG 9 supports industry, innovation and infrastructure through building resilient infrastructure, promoting sustainable industrialization and fostering innovation. Among its targets (9.1) is developing quality, reliable, sustainable and resilient infrastructure to support economic development and human well-being, with a focus on affordable and equitable access for all. Helping this target are ClearBridge holdings Vulcan Materials (VMC), which makes aggregates (such as gravel) for roads and other infrastructure, and United Rentals, which provides construction equipment such as scissor lifts, forklifts, loaders, excavators, hoists and cranes in a resource-efficient rental model that increases equipment utilization and reduces wasted capacity.

Resilient infrastructure may be supported by green building practices of real estate investment trusts like AvalonBay Communities (AVB), which owns and develops apartment properties. In a recent engagement with the company, we discussed AvalonBay’s submission to the Science-Based Targets initiative of a new emissions reduction target aligned to 1.5°C climate science, rather than its current 2°C target. This change, along with goals of 60% declines in Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions off a 2017 baseline by 2030, help ensure AvalonBay is bringing high sustainability standards to the ~300 communities and 91,000 homes it owns and develops. Other highlights of AvalonBay’s sustainable development include the use of embodied carbon emissions tracking (embodied emissions are those associated with the production stages of a product’s life), biodiversity and climate risk assessments, construction waste recycling and drought-tolerant and native plantings in its new development communities.

Electrification is also a key component of this target of SDG 9, and is supported by Eaton, whose electrical equipment enables the electrification of the power grid and, importantly, electrical vehicle charging infrastructure.

SDG 9 also includes a target (9.5) to enhance scientific research and upgrade the technological capabilities of industrial sectors in all countries and to increase the number of research and development workers per 1 million people. Semiconductors are a foundational technology for sustainable development. Almost every technology development in every sector is reliant on the development of better and better semiconductors, and many businesses ClearBridge owns and engages with are essential parts of that ecosystem:

  • Synopsys (SNPS) (which makes semiconductor design software)
  • ASML (ASML) and Lam Research (LRCX) (which make tools that produce semiconductor chips)
  • Nvidia and Broadcom (which design semiconductor chips)

These are also some of the most R&D-intensive businesses outside of health care. For example, every year Synopsys re-invests more than 30% of its revenue back into R&D to improve its next generation of chip design software. Also on this note, companies like Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) are innovating with research across multiple fields, including long-term research into areas like quantum computing.

The list could go on, but it is worth mentioning SDG 9’s target (9.c) to increase access to information and communications technology and strive to provide universal and affordable access to the Internet in least developed countries. Serving this goal too, then, is Amazon’s Project Kuiper, an initiative to use a network of low Earth orbit satellites to give affordable broadband access to underserved areas of the world without reliable connectivity.

Michael Kagan, Portfolio Manager

Stephen Rigo, CFA, Portfolio Manager

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Copyright © 2025 ClearBridge Investments. All opinions and data included in this commentary are as of the publication date and are subject to change. The opinions and views expressed herein are of the author and may differ from other portfolio managers or the firm as a whole, and are not intended to be a forecast of future events, a guarantee of future results or investment advice. This information should not be used as the sole basis to make any investment decision. The statistics have been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but the accuracy and completeness of this information cannot be guaranteed. Neither ClearBridge Investments, LLC nor its information providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information.

Performance source: Internal. Benchmark source: Standard & Poor's.

Original Post

data.title: DigitalOcean: Business Is Improving But Valuation Is Near All-Time Low
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about DigitalOcean (NYSE:DOCN) previously with a buy rating, as there are various growth drivers in place that should continue to support DOCN's growth outlook over the coming years, and my expectation was that will drive multiples upwards. The latest developments, in my view, reinforce my bull thesis. Product velocity is now clearly contributing to revenue, the new sales motion is working, and the GenAI platform potentially opens up a new layer of upside. With valuation still near historical lows, I think the setup remains attractive for long-term investors.

Product innovation is finally delivering on the revenue line

The crux of my bullish view was DOCN’s product innovation strategy. In my last post, I gave an overview of the key products available and why I think they would enable DOCN to capitalize on the AI demand trend. I think this aspect of DOCN’s equity story has gotten on two fronts:

  1. DOCN has doubled down on the pace of innovation, wherein DOCN product launches have accelerated meaningfully, from 42 product releases in 3Q24 to 49 in 4Q24 and 50+ in 1Q25 (management guidance). The incredible thing here is that these are not just minor patches but actual new products, as shown in the Investor Day; and
  2. The product velocity is translating into P&L contributions. It was disclosed that 27% of incremental revenue in 4Q24 came from products launched in 3Q24 and 4Q24. Even better, per the investor day, new features as a percentage of quarterly revenue improved sequentially, from 3.3% in 3Q24 to 4.1% in 4Q24, and are expected to be even higher in 1Q25. These are very important data points because we can now visualize the impact directly and have higher confidence that new products lead to revenue growth.

The last aspect that makes this entire process way better than it looks is that it was achieved without significantly ramping up R&D expense. For reference, R&D expense went up by ~$14 million, but product velocity increased almost 6x in FY24 (from investor day). If we assume this trend holds—new products being innovated with little incremental expense (due to the productivity of gen-AI) and these new products driving strong incremental revenue—it should put upward pressure on EBIT margin.

New go-to-market motions are starting to work

While the product story is encouraging, what’s also important is how DOCN is now able to better deliver these innovations to the right customers. Historically, DOCN relied almost entirely on a product-led growth [PLG] model, which is efficient because customers find DOCN when they need DOCN’s product to solve an issue. Basically, you can think of this as a self-serve/inbound motion where DOCN doesn’t really need a lot of boots on the ground to get sales. But this go-to-market [GTM] strategy is not always optimized for high-value accounts (large accounts tend to need more customization and more granularity on product terms like pricing, security, upgrades, etc.).

Things have changed. DOCN has now made multiple new GTM changes. For instance, DOCN grew its named account coverage from just 450 in 1H24 to 1,500 in 2H24 and is now targeting 3,000 accounts in 2025. These accounts are served by dedicated pods (with both commercial and technical account managers) and supported by a new migration services team. I like this setup because when each pod deals with a prospect, it is able to address all the needs, from pricing terms to technical aspects to migration. This end-to-end coverage makes it easier to close a deal since there is no need to redirect the prospect (a friction in the sales motion). Per management (source: investor day), this approach has already led to a 1,800 bps improvement in net dollar retention among $100k+ customers. This is a massive swing and a clear indicator that the model is working.

AI opportunity

Coming back to DOCN’s AI opportunity, I think the upside could be a lot more than I expected previously. The reason for this change in view is because in January 1Q25, DOCN rolled out its GenAI platform into public preview—which I think could be a game changer. Per the investor day, in just a few short weeks after the rollout, the platform has already attracted >2,000 customers who have built >6,000 agents (many of which are already in production). This is a very impressive stat, and if we look at the underlying use cases—invoice processing, help desk support, and customer engagement—it is very encouraging because it shows that this platform is actually helping customers improve productivity. I call these “real” use cases that are structural instead of “speculative” use cases like fully autonomous agents that manage complex business strategy.

If these users embed DOCN's platform deeper into their workflows, it could materially improve retention and pricing power. More importantly, it gives DOCN visibility into emerging usage patterns, which they can use to cross-sell products and expand wallet share. In that sense, the GenAI platform isn't just a new product — it could become a high-quality top-of-funnel engine.

Valuation

Despite all the fundamental progress, I thought DOCN deserved to see its valuation multiple move up instead of down. The only reason I can think of is that the market is in risk-off mode now due to Trump’s tariff actions, which have caused a lot of uncertainty in the markets. But if we take a more objective view, it seems logical to me that DOCN should trade higher from here when the macro environment normalizes. The business is clearly progressing in the right direction, but it is trading near its all-time low, which, if you look at the chart above, multiples tend to see a violent mean reversion when it reaches this low-4x level. I expect a similar pattern to happen as the market realizes DOCN business is improving and not deteriorating.

Risk

DOCN has exposure to SMB customers, and they tend to be more vulnerable to macro cycles or cost-cutting. If the macro backdrop gets worse, DOCN growth could see headwinds for the near term. Additionally, while early AI traction is promising, it’s still in public preview with no formal monetization disclosed. For all we know, DOCN may not be able to effectively monetize this.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for DOCN. The business is progressing well across all the right fronts—product, GTM, and platform expansion into GenAI. If execution continues on its current trajectory, with fundamentals strengthening and the stock still trading near trough valuation levels, I see the current share price as an attractive entry point.

data.title: Dollar General: Positioned To Perform Well Amidst Economic Uncertainty, Made Worse By Tariffs
data.description:
Introduction

Dollar General Corporation(NYSE:DG), the biggest dollar store retailer in the US, released its 2024 last March 13, 2025. In their report, the company continues to make progress in their back-to-basics in revitalizing their stores and improving shrink and supply chain efficiency initiatives. Furthermore, Dollar General management has provided guidance outlining its targets in growth, operating margin, EPS, and CAPEX. With the continued economic hardship facing Americans, especially the middle-income class, Dollar General is well-positioned to take advantage of the mid and high-income class trading down to their stores for quick and smaller-sized basket transactions. Furthermore, the recent tariff announcement by President Trump, according to economists, will further exacerbate financial strain among American consumers in the short term, which can benefit Dollar General as they will turn to smaller basket-size transactions.

Even with the stock rising to $87 recently, I will reiterate my 'Buy' rating for Dollar General as it's still selling at a lower multiple relative to retail leaders such as Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST). I believe Dollar General's leadership position in its own niche deserved a valuation similar to that of the retail industry leaders. Thus, I will maintain my 'Buy' rating for Dollar General.

2024 Results Takeaway

For the year 2024, Dollar General generated revenue of $40.6B, an increase of 4.5%, and same-store revenue growth of 1.4% from last year.

Operating profit plunged by 49.2%, from $579.7B last year to $294.2B this year. Relative to the previous fiscal year, operating profit declined by 29.9%, from $2.4B to $1.7B this year; this translates to an operating margin decline from 6.34% last year to 4.22% this year. Dollar General's operating margin has been on a downward trend since its peak in 2020.

Management attributed this decline to the impairment charges they incurred from its store optimization initiatives, mostly related to pOpshelf stores closure with impairment charges of around $232M, depreciation and amortization charges from new stores, and wage increases. Without the impairment charges, Dollar General's operating profit would've only declined by 20.91%, and its operating margin would've only declined to 4.79%. I'm not a big fan of impairment on assets since this results in artificially better performance that masks the bad decisions made by the management in the past. This is especially true if the management that invested an asset that got impaired is also the same one that decided to impair it, which in this case with pOpshelf, an initiative of Dollar General's current CEO Todd Davos, in 2020. Regardless, all management will make mistakes, and it's just part of the game in running a business. This impairment will improve Dollar General's future profitability due to the removal of depreciation & amortization expense from these underperforming pOpshelf assets.

Turning to the gross margin, Dollar General's gross margin declined by 0.7% from 30.29% last year to 29.59% this year due to significantly higher inventory shrinkage than in 2022. Unlike in previous years, Dollar General has specifically disclosed its inventory shrinkage for this year so investors can assess the severity of the problem, which is evident below.

The management is actively reducing shrinkage by converting self-checkout counters into personnel-assisted ones. The management believes that aside from preventing shrinkage, it will encourage consumer engagement, which is crucial for customer retention as they face increased competition from big-box retailers such as Walmart. While inventory shrinkage is sadly a natural cost of doing business for retailers, we cannot ignore the fact that Dollar General's shrinkage is significantly elevated relative to 2022 and is most likely out of the ordinary. Thus, it is reasonable to assume that this can be considered a one-time occurrence that can be fixed in the future with the initiatives that were mentioned earlier. If we assume the same level of shrinkage as 2022, which is around 1.3%(shrinkage / total revenue), Dollar General's gross margin in 2024 would be sitting at around 30.58%.

While Dollar General's pro-forma gross margin of 30.58% is still a few basis points below its historical level of around 31%, likely due to the elevated promotional environment indicated by the management in their 2024 Q2 earnings call, I expect gross margin to improve moving forward as promotional activities tone down. Furthermore, I am optimistic about the management's initiatives to increase non-consumables, which will contribute to gross margin expansion since it is a higher margin item for Dollar General when compared to consumables. The merchandise mix of Dollar General's sales currently stands at 82% consumables and 18% non-consumables. Historically, during good market conditions, Dollar General's revenue mix hovers at around 75%+ for consumables and 20%+ for non-consumables.

Dollar General's Future Financial Targets

In their Q4 2024 earnings call, Dollar General management outlined its financial targets for the coming years, which I believe is very conservative.

First, I consider their sales growth target very conservative, as it is barely above the US's usual GDP growth rate of around 2%. Management believes its initiatives to revitalize old stores will stimulate sales growth for the company moving forward.

Turning over to Dollar General's operating margin target. Aside from the impairments that will reduce future depreciation and amortization charges, management also aims to optimize its supply chain or improve the productivity of its distribution.

We are also working on tasks upstream, such as sorting in our distribution centers and case pack optimization, to allow our teams to get products to the shelf even quicker with less touches

Dollar General CEO Todd Vasos said in their Q4 2024 earnings call. While certainly there is efficiency that can be squeezed out of these initiatives, the biggest headwind for Dollar General's operating margin is its depreciation and amortization charges due to the rapid expansion the management has undertaken despite the slowing sales growth.
